figures.caption: TABLE I: Structural comparison between the macroscopic Filtered Navier–Stokes (FNSE) and the filtered Boltzmann equation (FBE), highlighting where SGS information is carried, how it is transported, and which contribution requires modeling. The detailed comparison in Table II specializes this structure to the BGK closure using the formal notation developed in Sec. IV.

TABLE II: Detailed structural comparison between the Filtered Navier–Stokes Equations (FNSE, Eq. (56)) and the Filtered Recorrelated BGK (FRBGK–BE, Eqs. (21) and (61)) framework. The advective closure hypothesis and the diffusive term hypothesis are listed as separate rows, reflecting conceptually independent modeling assumptions. In the FNSE, SGS transport requires an explicit advective closure (Boussinesq), whereas in the FRBGK–BE, the same information is exactly advected inside fsgs and the closure is relocated entirely to the collision side.
FIG. 1: Visual qualitative representation of the entropic descent in a simplified 3-component Hilbert space. The gray

FIG. 2: Time evolution of the normalized kinetic energy Ek/u2 0 (left) and enstrophy Z/(u0/LTGV)2 (right) for the Taylor–Green vortex at Re = 1600 and Ma = 0.2. Comparison between the high-resolution BGK reference simulation (N = 512), the residual-based closures (KC-RB and KC-MP), and the classical Smagorinsky models at varying resolutions.

FIG. 3: Time evolution of the normalized kinetic energy and enstrophy for the Taylor–Green vortex (Re = 1600). Comparison between the recursive kinetic closure (KC-RR) and the standard recursive regularization (RR) and hybrid recursive regularization (HRR) models.

FIG. 4: Turbulent kinetic energy spectra E(k) for the Taylor–Green vortex at tu0/LTGV = 10. Comparison between the residual-based closures (KC-RB and KC-MP), the Smagorinsky models, and the reference solution.
FIG. 5: Turbulent kinetic energy spectra for the comparison between KC-RR, RR, HRR, and KC-RB at tu0/LTGV = 10.

FIG. 6: Instantaneous volume renderings of the vorticity magnitude for the Taylor–Green vortex at Re = 1600 on the N = 64 lattice at tu0/LTGV ≈ 10. Plots (a)–(d) compare Smag. Naive, KC-RB, HRR (σ = 0.97), and KC-RR.

TABLE III: Aggregate root-mean-square deviations from the Leriche–Gavrilakis reference profiles [62] for the lid-driven cavity at Re = 12000 on the N = 128 lattice. The columns report the RMS errors of u′ x,rms/u0, u′ y,rms/u0, and 100⟨u′ xu′ y⟩/u2 0 along y/h; the last column is the arithmetic mean of the three errors. The table is restricted to N = 128, where all models are available as converged runs.

FIG. 7: Lid-driven-cavity root-mean-square statistics at Re = 12000 for the residual-based family. Profiles show u′ x,rms/u0, u′ y,rms/u0, and 100⟨u′ xu′ y⟩/u2 0 versus y/h. KC-RB (Cν = 0.02) and KC-MP (Cν = 0.035) are compared with the Smagorinsky models and the data of Leriche and Gavrilakis [62]. Top: N = 64; bottom: N = 128. The finite-difference Smagorinsky variant is marked unstable at N = 64 because it does not reach the averaging window.

FIG. 8: Lid-driven-cavity velocity-fluctuation statistics at Re = 12000 for KC-RR, RR, and HRR. Profiles show u′ x,rms/u0, u′ y,rms/u0, and 100⟨u′ xu′ y⟩/u2 0 versus y/h. KC-RR (Cν = 0.02) is compared with Recursive Regularization (RR) and Hybrid Recursive Regularization (HRR, σ = 0.97). Top: N = 64; bottom: N = 128. Leriche and Gavrilakis data [62] are shown as

FIG. 9: Instantaneous Q-criterion isosurfaces for the lid-driven cavity at Re = 12000 on the N = 128 lattice and tu0/h ≈ 450, with Qh2/u2 0 ≈ 1.23 (Q = 10−6 lu), colored by velocity magnitude. Panels (a)–(d) compare Smag. Naive, KC-RB, HRR

FIG. 10: Flow-past-cylinder time-averaged statistics at Re = 3900 for the residual-based kinetic closures. KC-RB and KC-MP are compared with the naive Smagorinsky model at D = 9.6 lattice cells. Profiles show the centerline mean streamwise velocity ⟨ux⟩/u0 along x/D, and ⟨ux⟩/u0, ⟨uy⟩/u0, and ⟨u′ xu′ x⟩/u2 0 at the near-wake station x/D = 1.562. High-resolution LES reference data from Parnaudeau et al. [65] are shown as the solid black line.

FIG. 11: Flow-past-cylinder time-averaged statistics at Re = 3900 for KC-RR and regularized collision models. KC-RR (Cν = 0.02) is compared with Recursive Regularization (RR) and Hybrid Recursive Regularization (HRR, σ = 0.97). Profiles show the centerline velocity and the near-wake distributions at x/D = 1.562.

TABLE IV: Aggregate root-mean-square deviations from the Parnaudeau et al. LES reference [65] for the flow past a circular cylinder at Re = 3900. The columns report the RMS errors of the centerline mean streamwise velocity profile ⟨ux⟩/u0, the

FIG. 12: Instantaneous Q-criterion isosurfaces for the cylinder wake at tu0/D ≈ 150, with QD2/u2 0 ≈ 0.691 (Q = 10−4 lu), colored by velocity magnitude. Panels (a)–(d) compare Smag. Naive, KC-RB, HRR (σ = 0.97), and KC-RR. KC-RB and KC-RR retain a denser small-scale wake than Smag. Naive and HRR.
TABLE V: Comparison between the collision term C of [43] and the present filtered collision-covariance source term E♯ cc. Here K, ε, and νT denote external macroscopic turbulent quantities in [43].
